verses.text: Early in his reign Saul keeps a standing army, and Jonathan strikes a Philistine garrison, stirring the whole conflict. Israel is summoned to Gilgal. The kingdom's first real test approaches — and it will reveal the king's heart far more than any battle will.

Early in his reign Saul keeps a standing army, and Jonathan strikes a Philistine garrison, stirring the whole conflict. Israel is summoned to Gilgal. The kingdom's first real test approaches — and it will reveal the king's heart far more than any battle will.

Early in his reign Saul keeps a standing army, and Jonathan strikes a Philistine garrison, stirring the whole conflict. Israel is summoned to Gilgal. The kingdom's first real test approaches — and it will reveal the king's heart far more than any battle will.

The Philistines muster a vast host, and the terrified Israelites scatter into caves and across the Jordan. Fear shrinks a people; the same enemy one army faces boldly, a faithless crowd flees. Where there is no trust in God, numbers and weapons cannot steady the heart.

The Philistines muster a vast host, and the terrified Israelites scatter into caves and across the Jordan. Fear shrinks a people; the same enemy one army faces boldly, a faithless crowd flees. Where there is no trust in God, numbers and weapons cannot steady the heart.

The Philistines muster a vast host, and the terrified Israelites scatter into caves and across the Jordan. Fear shrinks a people; the same enemy one army faces boldly, a faithless crowd flees. Where there is no trust in God, numbers and weapons cannot steady the heart.

Saul waits the appointed seven days, but as the army melts away and Samuel delays, he loses his nerve and offers the burnt offering himself — a thing no king might do. Impatience is the doorway to disobedience; he could not wait one hour more for God's man.

Saul waits the appointed seven days, but as the army melts away and Samuel delays, he loses his nerve and offers the burnt offering himself — a thing no king might do. Impatience is the doorway to disobedience; he could not wait one hour more for God's man.

Saul waits the appointed seven days, but as the army melts away and Samuel delays, he loses his nerve and offers the burnt offering himself — a thing no king might do. Impatience is the doorway to disobedience; he could not wait one hour more for God's man.

Challenged, Saul excuses himself: the people scattered, Samuel tarried, the enemy gathered, so 'I forced myself, and offered.' Every word is a reason, none is repentance. We are never more inventive than when defending a sin we have already chosen to commit.

Challenged, Saul excuses himself: the people scattered, Samuel tarried, the enemy gathered, so 'I forced myself, and offered.' Every word is a reason, none is repentance. We are never more inventive than when defending a sin we have already chosen to commit.

Samuel's verdict is heavy: 'thou hast done foolishly... now thy kingdom shall not continue.' God has sought 'a man after his own heart.' Obedience, not ceremony, is what God requires of a king; one act of self-willed disobedience forfeits a throne.

Samuel's verdict is heavy: 'thou hast done foolishly... now thy kingdom shall not continue.' God has sought 'a man after his own heart.' Obedience, not ceremony, is what God requires of a king; one act of self-willed disobedience forfeits a throne.

Samuel leaves, and Saul is left with barely six hundred men while Philistine raiders sweep the land in three companies. Disobedience never strengthens our position; it weakens it. The king who would not wait on God now faces the enemy stripped and exposed.

Samuel leaves, and Saul is left with barely six hundred men while Philistine raiders sweep the land in three companies. Disobedience never strengthens our position; it weakens it. The king who would not wait on God now faces the enemy stripped and exposed.

There was no smith in Israel — the Philistines saw to that — so on the day of battle only Saul and Jonathan had a sword or spear. A people who drift from God are slowly disarmed by their enemies. Yet God can save with empty hands, as the next chapter will show.

There was no smith in Israel — the Philistines saw to that — so on the day of battle only Saul and Jonathan had a sword or spear. A people who drift from God are slowly disarmed by their enemies. Yet God can save with empty hands, as the next chapter will show.

A Philistine garrison takes its post at the pass of Michmash, holding the high ground. The threat is real and near. But the scene is set not for Saul's strength to shine, but for the LORD to work a great deliverance through the faith of one man.
